# Certainty-Driven Autonomy

## Explains

Explains why the freedom given to an entity should be driven by task certainty, not by rank or position.

## The Concept

Traditional hierarchy gives more freedom to senior people regardless of task. This is backwards. A well-understood, repeatable task — filing a known form, running a standard deployment, applying a documented template — can be fully delegated to anyone (human or AI) regardless of position. The task itself is certain; guardrails are unnecessary because the path is known. Conversely, a novel, uncertain task — entering a new market, responding to an unprecedented failure, designing a system with unknown requirements — needs guardrails for everyone, including the CEO.

The variable that should determine delegation level is task certainty, not entity rank. This is a fundamental inversion of traditional management thinking. A junior engineer deploying a well-tested change to a well-understood system deserves full autonomy. A CTO making an architectural decision in an unexplored domain deserves structured challenge and review. The task's knowability, not the person's title, determines the appropriate governance intensity.

Cynefin's domains provide the framework for this: simple/clear tasks (high certainty) can be fully delegated with minimal oversight. Complicated tasks require expertise but are still knowable — delegate to competent entities with light verification. Complex tasks are unknowable in advance — require probing, sensing, and responding with tight feedback loops regardless of who is doing them. Chaotic tasks demand immediate action with post-hoc review. The governance response maps to the domain, not the actor.

## Why This Matters for Governance

This concept liberates governance from the assumption that seniority equals judgment. It means governance intensity should flex per-task, not per-person or per-role. A governance system that applies uniform oversight regardless of task certainty is wasting resources on known tasks while potentially under-governing uncertain ones.

For AI governance specifically, this is critical. An AI agent performing a well-understood, repeatable task with clear success criteria deserves high autonomy — human review adds latency without adding value. The same AI agent performing a novel, ambiguous task with unclear success criteria deserves tight human oversight. The agent's capability matters less than the task's certainty.

## Connects To

- JOS-C09 (Cynefin) — provides the domain classification that determines task certainty
- JOS-C16 (Trust Spectrum) — trust is built through demonstrated competence in tasks of increasing uncertainty
- JOS-P01 (Trust & Graduated Autonomy) — graduation should track task certainty, not just entity tenure
- JOS-P18 (Verification Proportional to Risk) — risk correlates with uncertainty; verification should too

## Provenance

Foundry FND-P02 "Task Certainty Drives Reasoning Freedom, Not Agent Rank", reframed as a universal governance and delegation principle. Influenced by Cynefin framework (Snowden), situational leadership theory (Hersey & Blanchard), and AI alignment research on capability-appropriate oversight.
